rel="preconnect" href="https://ads-static.conde.digital"/><script id="ads-v6" src="https://ads-static.conde.digital/production/cns/builds/wired/v6.js?v=12295" async=""></script><script type="application/ld+json">{"@context":"http://schema.org","@type":"NewsArticle","articleBody":"The latest example of a VPN meltdown—we’re talking corporate connections, not your personal setup—is among the most dramatic. Security firm FireEye this week revealed that it had found a dozen malware families, spread across multiple hacking groups, feasting on vulnerabilities in Pulse Secure VPN. The victims spanned the globe and ranged across the usual high-value targets: defense contractors, financial institutions, and governments. The attackers used their perch to steal legitimate credentials, improving their chances of gaining access that’s both deep and sustained.\nWhich is the thing about VPN hacks. Since the whole point of a VPN is to create a secure connection to a network, worming into one can save hackers a lot of hassle. “Once hackers have those credentials, they don’t need to use spearphishing emails, they don’t need to bring in custom malware,” says Sarah Jones, senior principal analyst at FireEye. “It’s kind of a perfect situation.”\nThe campaign that FireEye uncovered is especially ambitious and potentially troubling. It’s too early for firm attribution, but the groups behind it appear to be linked to China, and their targets seem chock full of the kind of sensitive information on which espionage groups thrive. One of the malware families, called Slowpulse, could get around two-factor authentication protections, sidestepping a key safeguard against credential harvesting.\n“The new issue, discovered this month, impacted a very limited number of customers,” said Pulse Secure parent company Ivanti in a statement. “The team worked quickly to provide mitigations directly to the limited number of impacted customers that remediates the risk to their system.”\nA patch to fix the vulnerability at the heart of the attacks, though, won’t be available until next month. And even then, it may not provide much of a salve. Companies are often slow to update their VPNs, in part because downtime means employees effectively can’t get their work done. Some of the intrusions FireEye spotted, in fact, appear related to vulnerabilities that had been reported as far back as 2019. That same year, a Pulse Secure VPN flaw offered an inroad for a ransomware group to hold up Travelex, a travel insurance company, for millions of dollars. A year later—despite warnings from researchers, national cybersecurity organizations, and law enforcement—thousands of organizations remained vulnerable, says Troy Mursch, chief research officer of the cyber-threat intelligence company Bad Packets.\nIt wasn’t always like this. VPNs used to typically rely on a set of protocols known as Internet Protocol Security, or IPsec. While IPsec-based VPNs are considered secure and reliable, they can also be complicated and clunky for users. In recent years, as remote work expanded then exploded, more and more VPNs have been built instead on ubiquitous encryption technologies known as secure sockets layer and transport layer security. The distinctions descend rapidly into weeds, but essentially SSL/TLS VPNs made logging onto your company’s network much more seamless—the difference between merging onto the interstate in a minivan versus a Miata.\n“That was a big step for convenience,” says Vijay Sarvepalli, a senior security solutions architect with the CERT Coordination Center at Carnegie Mellon University. CERT helps catalog vulnerabilities and coordinate their public disclosure. “When they designed those things, the risks were not yet considered. It’s not impossible to protect these, but people are not prepared to monitor and respond quickly to attacks against them.”\nSoftware of all stripes have vulnerabilities, but because VPNs by definition act as a conduit for information that’s intended to be private, their bugs have serious implications. The pandemic's shift to remote work has thrust the underlying issues into the spotlight. “Many SSL VPN vendors had serious flaws in their products to begin with,” says Mursch. “The increased usage of SSL VPNs over the last year led to more scrutiny from security researchers—and threat actors interested in exploiting them.”\nMore flaws means more opportunities for attackers. More users on a given VPN also makes it that much more difficult to spot the bad guys, especially for large, multinational companies. “It makes it harder to monitor when you have a lot more events going on,” says Sarvepalli. “If the haystack keeps increasing, it’s impossible to find the needle.”\nTo the extent that there’s a silver lining here, it’s that the hackers behind the latest set of Pulse Secure-related intrusions are incredibly sophisticated. OK, yes, that’s also bad news, in that they’ve likely stolen sensitive data from who knows how many corners of the world. But it also means that copycats are unlikely to be able to replicate their moves before the vulnerability gets patched, at least not without a lot of work.\n“It’s rather specific in the knowledge you need to exploit it,” say Stephen Eckels, a reverse engineer at FireEye. “For us to understand what their malware was doing, we had to be in contact with the authors of the code from Pulse Secure. An attacker was able to figure out that same information on their own. ”\nStill, VPN vulnerabilities that are easier to exploit persist. Hackers will eventually reverse engineer this one after the patch comes out. And corporations continue not to address the exposure to their networks, despite frantic warnings from the security community. That status quo adds up to months or years of quiet espionage, the kind that may never lend itself to a full accounting. “There’s a lot to be done still,” says Sarvepalli of the work required to shore up VPNs. “We are making progress, and some of these big hammers wake us up.”\n\nMore Great WIRED Stories\n\n📩 The latest on tech, science, and more: Get our newsletters!\nThe cold war over McDonald's hacked ice cream machines\nWhat octopus dreams tell us about the evolution of sleep\nThe lazy gamer’s guide to cable management\nHow to log in to your devices without passwords\nHelp! Am I oversharing with my colleagues?\n👁️ Explore AI like never before with our new database\n🎮 WIRED Games: Get the latest tips, reviews, and more\n🏃🏽‍♀️ Want the best tools to get healthy? Check out our Gear team’s picks for the best fitness trackers, running gear (including shoes and socks), and best               if (!window[r]) {
                let initArgs, realInit, pending = [];

                const cdn =

          </script><script type="text/javascript" data-testid="growthbook-script">(function (props) {
        const { userAuthenticated, section, contentType, hasBuyButtons, clientSdkKey, apiHost, enableClient, brandSlug } = props;
        const CookieAttributes = {
            AMG_USER_PARTNER: 'amg_user_partner',
            CN_XID: 'CN_xid',
            CN_SEGMENTS: 'CN_segments',
            CN_GEO_COUNTRY_CODE: 'CN_geo_country_code',
            CN_DEBUG: 'cn-debug'
        };
        function extractExperimentData(experiment, result) {
            return {
                experiment_id: experiment.key,
                feature_id: result.featureId || '',
                variation: result.key,
                variation_name: result.name || '',
                payload: result.value
            };
        }
        function updateDataLayer(expDataLayer) {
            if (!Array.isArray(window.dataLayer) ||
                typeof window.dataLayer[0] !== 'object' ||
                expDataLayer.length === 0) {
                return;
            }
            const mergeAssignments = (newAssignments = [], existingAssignments = []) => {
                const merged = [...existingAssignments];
                newAssignments.forEach((newAssignment) => {
                    const existingIndex = merged.findIndex((existing) => existing.experiment_id === newAssignment.experiment_id);
                    if (existingIndex >= 0) {
                        merged[existingIndex] = newAssignment;
                    }
                    else {
                        merged.push(newAssignment);
                    }
                });
                return merged;
            };
            window.dataLayer[0].experiments = mergeAssignments(expDataLayer, window.dataLayer[0].experiments || []);
            if (window.cns) {
                const { experiments } = window.dataLayer[0];
                const { pageContext } = window.cns;
                if (experiments && experiments.length) {
                    pageContext.experiments = formatExperimentsForPageContext(experiments);
                }
            }
            const kendraGrowthBook = window.Kendra.GROWTHBOOK;
            if (kendraGrowthBook && kendraGrowthBook.broadcast) {
                kendraGrowthBook.broadcast('experiments.assignments.processed');
            }
        }
        function formatExperimentsForPageContext(experiments) {
            if (!experiments.length) {
                return {};
            }
            const bucketLabel = [];
            const experimentLabel = [];
            const payloadArray = [];
            experiments.forEach((experiment) => {
                const { variation_name, experiment_id, payload } = experiment;
                bucketLabel.push(`${variation_name}`);
                experimentLabel.push(`${experiment_id}`);
                if (Array.isArray(payload)) {
                    payloadArray.push(...payload);
                }
                else if (payload) {
                    payloadArray.push(payload);
                }
            });
            return {
                bucketLabel,
                experimentLabel,
                payload: payloadArray
            };
        }
        function perfMark(name) {
            if (typeof window.performance !== 'undefined' &&
                typeof window.performance.mark !== 'undefined') {
                window.performance.mark(name);
            }
        }
        function perfMeasure(name, startMark, endMark) {
            if (typeof window.performance !== 'undefined' &&
                typeof window.performance.measure !== 'undefined') {
                try {
                    window.performance.measure(name, startMark, endMark);
                }
                catch (e) {
                    console.error(`Error setting performance measure: ${e}`);
                }
            }
        }
        function getCookie(name) {
            if (typeof document === 'undefined' || !name) {
                return '';
            }
            const cookiePieces = document.cookie.split(/;\s?/);
            let cookieValue = '';
            for (let i = 0; i < cookiePieces.length; i++) {
                const parts = cookiePieces[i].split('=');
                if (parts[0] === name) {
                    cookieValue = decodeURIComponent(parts.slice(1).join('='));
                    break;
                }
            }
            return cookieValue;
        }
        const isDesktopBrowser = () => {
            const { userAgent } = navigator;
            return !/Android|webOS|iPhone|iPad|iPod|BlackBerry|IEMobile|Opera Mini/i.test(userAgent);
        };
        const localStorageXidCleanUp = (prefix, xid) => {
            if (!xid) {
                return;
            }
            for (let i = 0; i < localStorage.length; i++) {
                const key = window.localStorage.key(i);
                if (key && key.startsWith(prefix) && !key.includes(xid)) {
                    window.localStorage.removeItem(key);
                }
            }
        };
        const isMobile = !isDesktopBrowser();
        const userXid = getCookie(CookieAttributes.CN_XID);
        try {
            const segment = getCookie(CookieAttributes.CN_SEGMENTS) || null;
            const amgUserPartner = getCookie(CookieAttributes.AMG_USER_PARTNER) || '';
            const countryCode = getCookie(CookieAttributes.CN_GEO_COUNTRY_CODE) || '';
            window.growthbook_config = window.growthbook_config || {};
            const { userAgent } = navigator;
            window.growthbook_config.attributes = {
                userAgent,
                id: userXid,
                url: window.location.href,
                isMobile,
                loggedIn: userAuthenticated,
                segment,
                section,
                contentType,
                hasBuyButtons,
                amgUserPartner,
                countryCode,
                brandSlug
            };
            window.growthbook_config.trackingCallback = (experiment, result) => {
                const expDataLayer = [];
                const experimentData = extractExperimentData(experiment, result);
                const currentExp = {
                    ...experimentData,
                    status: 'CLIENT_ASSIGNMENT'
                };
                expDataLayer.push(currentExp);
                updateDataLayer(expDataLayer);
                perfMark(`gb-script-tracking-end`);
                perfMeasure(`gb-script-tracking-finish`, `gb-script-end`, `gb-script-tracking-end`);
            };
            window.growthbook_queue = window.growthbook_queue || [];
            window.growthbook_queue.push((gb) => {
                const checkReady = () => {
                    if (gb.ready) {
                        perfMark(`gb-script-end`);
                        perfMeasure(`gb-script-ready`, `gb-script-start`, `gb-script-end`);
                    }
                    else {
                        setTimeout(checkReady, 0);
                    }
                };
                checkReady();
            });
        }
        catch (e) {
            console.error('GrowthBook config setup error:', e);
        }
        try {
            const stickyBrands = ['tatler', 'woi'];
            const isStickyBucketingEnabled = stickyBrands.includes(brandSlug) && !!userXid;
            if (isStickyBucketingEnabled) {
                localStorageXidCleanUp('gbStickyBuckets__', userXid);
            }
            if (clientSdkKey && apiHost && enableClient && userXid) {
                const cnDebug = getCookie(CookieAttributes.CN_DEBUG) || false;
                const s = document.createElement('script');
                s.async = true;
                s.dataset.clientKey = clientSdkKey;
                s.dataset.apiHost = apiHost;
                if (isStickyBucketingEnabled) {
                    s.dataset.useStickyBucketService = 'localStorage';
                    s.dataset.stickyBucketPrefix = 'gbStickyBuckets__';
                }
                s.dataset.uuid = userXid;
                s.dataset.enableDevMode = cnDebug.toString();
                s.src = `/verso/static/assets/growthbook.min.js`;
                document.head.appendChild(s);
                perfMark(`gb-script-start`);
            }
            else {
                console.warn('SDK loading skipped - missing requirements:', {
                    hasClientSdkKey: !!clientSdkKey,
                    hasApiHost: !!apiHost,
                    enableClient,
                    hasExpId: !!userXid
                });
            }
        }
        catch (e) {
            console.error('GrowthBook SDK loading error:', e);
        }

ContentHeaderLeadAssetCaption-gegwVb gfDuTb" data-testid="caption-wrapper" topSpacing="1"><span class="BaseText-fEwdHD CaptionText-cQpRdU kRTNAB hbiMYj caption__text">Hackers have had a field day with virtual private networks—especially during the remote-work pandemic era.</span><span class="BaseText-fEwdHD CaptionCredit-cUgOGk iQbGEh hRFzlA caption__credit" creditTextStyle="default">Illustration: Elena Lacey</span></div></div></figure><div data-testid="ContentHeaderLeadRailAnchor" class="ContentHeaderLeadRailAnchor-eeUgSm iKYbcs"></div></div></div></header></div></div><div cartoonVariation="default" data-attribute-verso-pattern="article-body" class="ArticlePageContentBackGround-eDdQYX jBrCot article-body__content"><div data-testid="action-bar-wrapper" actionBarHeight="0" class="ActionBarWrapperContent-eyqXkK edhert"><div class="ActionBarWrapperComponent-chDeHg dIFaKt"><div data-attr-viewport-monitor="" class="ActionBarWrapper-kJGqdz dwThYB viewport-monitor-anchor"><button id="bookmark" aria-label="Save this story" class="ActionBarButton-fDeyRu iyYvdU bookmark large-screen"><span class="ActionBarSecondaryButtonPrimaryIcon-bHDDJb lSyOq bookmark-button-icon" count="0"><svg class="icon icon-bookmark" width="24" height="24" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><title>Save Story</title><path d="M11.6279 17.2126L5 21V4H19V21L12.3721 17.2126L12 17L11.6279 17.2126Z" stroke="black" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="square" class="icon-bookmark-stroke" fill="none"></path></svg></span><span class="BaseText-fEwdHD ActionBarButtonText-cQLtCT dfgfZR bJJzeS">Save this story</span></button></div><div data-attr-viewport-monitor="" class="ActionBarWrapper-kJGqdz klCXQy viewport-monitor-anchor"><button id="bookmark" aria-label="Save this story" class="ActionBarButton-fDeyRu gYVqLg bookmark mobile" maxScreenThreshold="lg"><span class="ActionBarSecondaryButtonPrimaryIcon-bHDDJb lSyOq bookmark-button-icon" count="0"><svg class="icon icon-bookmark" width="24" height="24" fill="none" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><title>Save Story</title><path d="M11.6279 17.2126L5 21V4H19V21L12.3721 17.2126L12 17L11.6279 17.2126Z" stroke="black" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-linecap="square" class="icon-bookmark-stroke" fill="none"></path></svg></span><span class="BaseText-fEwdHD ActionBarButtonText-cQLtCT dfgfZR bJJzeS">Save this story</span></button></div></div></div><div class="ArticlePageChunksContent-hsbblt lnfIPJ"><div data-testid="ArticlePageChunks" class="ArticlePageChunks-lfyGNk gEPfMM"><div class="GridWrapper-uulHE dCnZHU grid grid-margins grid-items-2 ArticlePageChunksGrid-kSknMg dSvDdx grid-layout--adrail narrow wide-adrail" data-journey-hook="grid-wrapper"><div class="GridItem-bufwYC kKTABR grid--item grid-layout__content"><div class="BodyWrapper-cuqCAc eujiPN body body__container article__body" data-journey-hook="client-content" data-testid="BodyWrapper"><div class="body__inner-container"><p><span class="lead-in-text-callout">This year has</span> seen no shortage of blockbuster hacks, from the <a href="https://www.wired.com/story/us-russia-sanctions-solarwinds-svr/">SolarWinds supply chain meltdown</a> to <a href="https://www.wired.com/story/china-microsoft-exchange-server-hack-victims/">China’s blitz against Microsoft Exchange servers</a>. It’s a lot. But the outsized focus on those hacking sprees obscures another threat that has built steadily in the background for years, with no clean resolution in sight: the sustained assault on virtual private networks.</p><p class="paywall">The latest example of a VPN meltdown—we’re talking corporate connections, not <a href="https://www.wired.com/story/best-vpn/">your personal setup</a>—is among the most dramatic. Security firm FireEye this week revealed that it had found a dozen malware families, spread across multiple hacking groups, feasting on vulnerabilities in Pulse Secure VPN. The victims spanned the globe and ranged across the usual high-value targets: defense contractors, financial institutions, and governments. The attackers used their perch to steal legitimate credentials, improving their chances of gaining access that’s both deep and sustained. </p><div></div><div></div><p class="paywall">Which is the thing about VPN hacks. Since the whole point of a VPN is to create a secure connection to a network, worming into one can save hackers a lot of hassle. “Once hackers have those credentials, they don’t need to use spearphishing emails, they don’t need to bring in custom malware,” says Sarah Jones, senior principal analyst at FireEye. “It’s kind of a perfect situation.”</p><div class="Container-ioqmpI jzsvml"></div><p class="paywall">The campaign that FireEye uncovered is especially ambitious and potentially troubling. It’s too early for firm attribution, but the groups behind it appear to be linked to China, and their targets seem chock full of the kind of sensitive information on which espionage groups thrive. One of the malware families, called Slowpulse, could get around two-factor authentication protections, sidestepping a key safeguard against credential harvesting. </p><p class="paywall">“The new issue, discovered this month, impacted a very limited number of customers,” said Pulse Secure parent company Ivanti in a statement. “The team worked quickly to provide mitigations directly to the limited number of impacted customers that remediates the risk to their system.” </p><div class="AdWrapper-fGiLaV iGDxYx ad ad--sponsor-product"><div class="ad__slot ad__slot--sponsor-product" data-node-id="k0a6ov"></div></div><aside aria-hidden="true" textAlign="left" data-testid="pullquote-embed" class="PullQuoteEmbedWrapper-sc-efqGLV gxAcZF"><div class="PullQuoteDecorativeBorder-sc-gvnuIp MpaHa"></div><div contentType="pullquoteContent" class="PullQuoteEmbedContent-sc-fzpWpD hsyaOf"><p class="paywall">“If the haystack keeps increasing, it’s impossible to find the needle.” </p></div><div contentType="pullquoteCredit" class="PullQuoteEmbedCredit-sc-LySNH djUXBQ"><span class="paywall">Vijay Sarvepalli, CERT</span></div></aside><div class="AdWrapper-fGiLaV iGDxYx ad ad--in-content"><div class="ad__slot ad__slot--in-content" data-node-id="bf4oas"></div></div><p class="paywall">A patch to fix the vulnerability at the heart of the attacks, though, won’t be available until next month. And even then, it may not provide much of a salve. Companies are often slow to update their VPNs, in part because downtime means employees effectively can’t get their work done. Some of the intrusions FireEye spotted, in fact, appear related to vulnerabilities that had been reported as far back as 2019. That same year, a Pulse Secure VPN flaw offered an inroad for a ransomware group to hold up Travelex, a travel insurance company, <a data-offer-url="https://www.wsj.com/articles/major-companies-shared-vulnerability-used-in-travelex-cyberattack-11579177596" class="external-link" data-event-click="{&quot;element&quot;:&quot;ExternalLink&quot;,&quot;outgoingURL&quot;:&quot;https://www.wsj.com/articles/major-companies-shared-vulnerability-used-in-travelex-cyberattack-11579177596&quot;}" href="https://www.wsj.com/articles/major-companies-shared-vulnerability-used-in-travelex-cyberattack-11579177596" rel="nofollow noopener" target="_blank">for millions of dollars</a>. A year later—despite warnings from researchers, national cybersecurity organizations, and law enforcement—thousands of organizations remained vulnerable, says Troy Mursch, chief research officer of the cyber-threat intelligence company Bad Packets.</p></div></div></div><div class="GridItem-bufwYC fdedvq grid--item grid-layout__aside"></div></div><div class="GridWrapper-uulHE dCnZHU grid grid-margins grid-items-2 ArticlePageChunksGrid-kSknMg dSvDdx grid-layout--adrail narrow wide-adrail" data-journey-hook="grid-wrapper"><div class="GridItem-bufwYC kKTABR grid--item grid-layout__content"><div class="BodyWrapper-cuqCAc eujiPN body body__container article__body" data-journey-hook="client-content" data-testid="BodyWrapper"><div class="body__inner-container"><p class="paywall">It wasn’t always like this. VPNs used to typically rely on a set of protocols known as Internet Protocol Security, or IPsec. While IPsec-based VPNs are considered secure and reliable, they can also be complicated and clunky for users. In recent years, as remote work expanded then exploded, more and more VPNs have been built instead on ubiquitous encryption technologies known as secure sockets layer and transport layer security. The distinctions descend rapidly into weeds, but essentially SSL/TLS VPNs made logging onto your company’s network much more seamless—the difference between merging onto the interstate in a minivan versus a Miata.</p><div><div class="ConsumerMarketingUnitThemedWrapper-kjbXjp gnOkbl consumer-marketing-unit consumer-marketing-unit--article-mid-content" role="presentation" aria-hidden="true"><div class="consumer-marketing-unit__slot consumer-marketing-unit__slot--article-mid-content consumer-marketing-unit__slot--in-content"></div><div class="journey-unit"></div></div></div><p class="paywall">“That was a big step for convenience,” says Vijay Sarvepalli, a senior security solutions architect with the CERT Coordination Center at Carnegie Mellon University. CERT helps catalog vulnerabilities and coordinate their public disclosure. “When they designed those things, the risks were not yet considered. It’s not impossible to protect these, but people are not prepared to monitor and respond quickly to attacks against them.”</p><p class="paywall">Software of all stripes have vulnerabilities, but because VPNs by definition act as a conduit for information that’s intended to be private, their bugs have serious implications. The pandemic&#x27;s shift to remote work has thrust the underlying issues into the spotlight. “Many SSL VPN vendors had serious flaws in their products to begin with,” says Mursch. “The increased usage of SSL VPNs over the last year led to more scrutiny from security researchers—and threat actors interested in exploiting them.” </p><p class="paywall">More flaws means more opportunities for attackers. More users on a given VPN also makes it that much more difficult to spot the bad guys, especially for large, multinational companies. “It makes it harder to monitor when you have a lot more events going on,” says Sarvepalli. “If the haystack keeps increasing, it’s impossible to find the needle.” </p><p class="paywall">To the extent that there’s a silver lining here, it’s that the hackers behind the latest set of Pulse Secure-related intrusions are incredibly sophisticated. OK, yes, that’s also bad news, in that they’ve likely stolen sensitive data from who knows how many corners of the world. But it also means that copycats are unlikely to be able to replicate their moves before the vulnerability gets patched, at least not without a lot of work.</p><p class="paywall">“It’s rather specific in the knowledge you need to exploit it,” say Stephen Eckels, a reverse engineer at FireEye. “For us to understand what their malware was doing, we had to be in contact with the authors of the code from Pulse Secure. An attacker was able to figure out that same information on their own. ”</p><p class="paywall">Still, VPN vulnerabilities that are easier to exploit persist. Hackers will eventually reverse engineer this one after the patch comes out. And corporations continue not to address the exposure to their networks, despite frantic warnings from the security community. That status quo adds up to months or years of quiet espionage, the kind that may never lend itself to a full accounting. “There’s a lot to be done still,” says Sarvepalli of the work required to shore up VPNs. “We are making progress, and some of these big hammers wake us up.”</p><hr class="paywall"/><div role="heading" aria-level="3" class="paywall heading-h3">More Great WIRED Stories</div><ul class="paywall"><li>📩 The latest on tech, science, and more: <a href="https://www.wired.com/newsletter?sourceCode=BottomStories">Get our newsletters</a>!</li><li>The cold war over McDonald&#x27;s <a href="https://www.wired.com/story/they-hacked-mcdonalds-ice-cream-makers-started-cold-war/?itm_campaign=BottomRelatedStories_Sections_1&amp;itm_content=footer-recirc">hacked ice cream machines</a></li><li>What octopus dreams tell us about the <a href="https://www.wired.com/story/what-octopus-dreams-tell-us-about-the-evolution-of-sleep/?itm_campaign=BottomRelatedStories_Sections_1&amp;itm_content=footer-recirc">evolution of sleep</a></li><li>The lazy gamer’s <a href="https://www.wired.com/story/lazy-gamers-guide-cable-management/?itm_campaign=BottomRelatedStories_Sections_1&amp;itm_content=footer-recirc">guide to cable management</a></li><li>How to log in to your devices <a href="https://www.wired.com/story/how-to-log-in-without-passwords-touchid-face-unlock-windows-hello/?itm_campaign=BottomRelatedStories_Sections_1&amp;itm_content=footer-recirc">without passwords</a></li><li>Help! It’s a lot. But the outsized focus on those hacking sprees obscures another threat that has built steadily in the background for years, with no clean resolution in sight: the sustained assault on virtual private networks."],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"The latest example of a VPN meltdown—we’re talking corporate connections, not ",["a",{"href":"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.wired.com\u002Fstory\u002Fbest-vpn\u002F","isExternal":false},"your personal setup"],"—is among the most dramatic. Security firm FireEye this week revealed that it had found a dozen malware families, spread across multiple hacking groups, feasting on vulnerabilities in Pulse Secure VPN. The victims spanned the globe and ranged across the usual high-value targets: defense contractors, financial institutions, and governments. The attackers used their perch to steal legitimate credentials, improving their chances of gaining access that’s both deep and sustained. "],["journey-inline-newsletter",{"newsletterType":"slim-newsletter","newsletters":[]}],["inline-newsletter",{"newsletterType":"slim-newsletter","newsletters":[]}],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"Which is the thing about VPN hacks. Since the whole point of a VPN is to create a secure connection to a network, worming into one can save hackers a lot of hassle. “Once hackers have those credentials, they don’t need to use spearphishing emails, they don’t need to bring in custom malware,” says Sarah Jones, senior principal analyst at FireEye. “It’s kind of a perfect situation.”"],["inline-embed",{"type":"cneinterlude","props":{"brand":"wired","embeddedVideos":[],"hasExcludedEmbed":false,"playerBase":"https:\u002F\u002Fplayer.cnevids.com","isTitleBelowVideo":true,"isTitleLeftAligned":true,"isRailEligible":false,"isRightRail":false,"relatedVideo":{"showRelatedVideo":true,"brand":"wired","related":{"id":"5dc42cfd8ebd000007e144fc","animationClip":{"highResUrl":null,"lowResUrl":null},"categories":["Technology"],"cneId":"5dc3378934e7946ad26c644f","hed":"Cybersecurity Expert Answers Hacking Questions From Twitter  ","metadata":{"contentType":"cnevideo"},"scriptEmbedUrl":"\u002F\u002Fplayer.cnevids.com\u002Fscript\u002Fvideo\u002F5dc3378934e7946ad26c644f.js","title":"Cybersecurity Expert Answers Hacking Questions From Twitter  ","url":"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.wired.com\u002Fvideo\u002Fwatch\u002Ftech-support-hacker-answers-hacking-questions-from-twitter","photos":{"cnevideo":[{"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fdwgyu36up6iuz.cloudfront.net\u002Fheru80fdn\u002Fimage\u002Fupload\u002Fc_fill,d_placeholder_thescene.jpg,fl_progressive,g_center,h_360,q_80,w_640\u002Fv1573076979\u002Fwired_tech-support-hacker-answers-hacking-questions-from-twitter.jpg"}]},"modelName":"cnevideo","meta":{"modelName":"cnevideo"}},"pageSize":12}}}],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"The campaign that FireEye uncovered is especially ambitious and potentially troubling. It’s too early for firm attribution, but the groups behind it appear to be linked to China, and their targets seem chock full of the kind of sensitive information on which espionage groups thrive. One of the malware families, called Slowpulse, could get around two-factor authentication protections, sidestepping a key safeguard against credential harvesting. "],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"“The new issue, discovered this month, impacted a very limited number of customers,” said Pulse Secure parent company Ivanti in a statement. “The team worked quickly to provide mitigations directly to the limited number of impacted customers that remediates the risk to their system.” "],["native-ad",{"position":"sponsor-product","shouldDisplayLabel":true}],["inline-embed",{"props":{"name":"pullquote","attrs":{},"childTypes":["pullquoteContent","pullquoteCredit"]},"ref":"","type":"callout:pullquote"},["inline-embed",{"props":{},"type":"pullquoteContent"},["p",{"class":"paywall"},"“If the haystack keeps increasing, it’s impossible to find the needle.” "]],["inline-embed",{"props":{},"type":"pullquoteCredit"},["span",{"class":"paywall"},"Vijay Sarvepalli, CERT"]]],["native-ad",{"position":"in-content","shouldDisplayLabel":true}],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"A patch to fix the vulnerability at the heart of the attacks, though, won’t be available until next month. And even then, it may not provide much of a salve. Companies are often slow to update their VPNs, in part because downtime means employees effectively can’t get their work done. Some of the intrusions FireEye spotted, in fact, appear related to vulnerabilities that had been reported as far back as 2019. That same year, a Pulse Secure VPN flaw offered an inroad for a ransomware group to hold up Travelex, a travel insurance company, ",["a",{"href":"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.wsj.com\u002Farticles\u002Fmajor-companies-shared-vulnerability-used-in-travelex-cyberattack-11579177596","rel":"nofollow noopener","attributes":{"data-offer-url":"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.wsj.com\u002Farticles\u002Fmajor-companies-shared-vulnerability-used-in-travelex-cyberattack-11579177596"},"isExternal":true},"for millions of dollars"],". A year later—despite warnings from researchers, national cybersecurity organizations, and law enforcement—thousands of organizations remained vulnerable, says Troy Mursch, chief research officer of the cyber-threat intelligence company Bad Packets."],["ad",{"position":"mid-content"}],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"It wasn’t always like this. VPNs used to typically rely on a set of protocols known as Internet Protocol Security, or IPsec. While IPsec-based VPNs are considered secure and reliable, they can also be complicated and clunky for users. In recent years, as remote work expanded then exploded, more and more VPNs have been built instead on ubiquitous encryption technologies known as secure sockets layer and transport layer security. The distinctions descend rapidly into weeds, but essentially SSL\u002FTLS VPNs made logging onto your company’s network much more seamless—the difference between merging onto the interstate in a minivan versus a Miata."],["cm-unit"],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"“That was a big step for convenience,” says Vijay Sarvepalli, a senior security solutions architect with the CERT Coordination Center at Carnegie Mellon University. CERT helps catalog vulnerabilities and coordinate their public disclosure. “When they designed those things, the risks were not yet considered. It’s not impossible to protect these, but people are not prepared to monitor and respond quickly to attacks against them.”"],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"Software of all stripes have vulnerabilities, but because VPNs by definition act as a conduit for information that’s intended to be private, their bugs have serious implications. The pandemic's shift to remote work has thrust the underlying issues into the spotlight. “Many SSL VPN vendors had serious flaws in their products to begin with,” says Mursch. “The increased usage of SSL VPNs over the last year led to more scrutiny from security researchers—and threat actors interested in exploiting them.” "],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"More flaws means more opportunities for attackers. More users on a given VPN also makes it that much more difficult to spot the bad guys, especially for large, multinational companies. “It makes it harder to monitor when you have a lot more events going on,” says Sarvepalli. “If the haystack keeps increasing, it’s impossible to find the needle.” "],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"To the extent that there’s a silver lining here, it’s that the hackers behind the latest set of Pulse Secure-related intrusions are incredibly sophisticated. OK, yes, that’s also bad news, in that they’ve likely stolen sensitive data from who knows how many corners of the world. But it also means that copycats are unlikely to be able to replicate their moves before the vulnerability gets patched, at least not without a lot of work."],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"“It’s rather specific in the knowledge you need to exploit it,” say Stephen Eckels, a reverse engineer at FireEye. “For us to understand what their malware was doing, we had to be in contact with the authors of the code from Pulse Secure. An attacker was able to figure out that same information on their own. ”"],["p",{"class":"paywall"},"Still, VPN vulnerabilities that are easier to exploit persist. Hackers will eventually reverse engineer this one after the patch comes out. And corporations continue not to address the exposure to their networks, despite frantic warnings from the security community. That status quo adds up to months or years of quiet espionage, the kind that may never lend itself to a full accounting. “There’s a lot to be done still,” says Sarvepalli of the work required to shore up VPNs. “We are making progress, and some of these big hammers wake us up.”"],["hr",{"class":"paywall"}],["div",{"class":"paywall heading-h3","role":"heading","aria-level":"3"},"More Great WIRED Stories"],["ul",{"class":"paywall"},["li","📩 The latest on tech, science, and more: ",["a",{"href":"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.wired.com\u002Fnewsletter?sourceCode=BottomStories","isExternal":false},"Get our newsletters"],"!"],["li","The cold war over McDonald's ",["a",{"href":"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.wired.com\u002Fstory\u002Fthey-hacked-mcdonalds-ice-cream-makers-started-cold-war\u002F?itm_campaign=BottomRelatedStories_Sections_1&itm_content=footer-recirc","isExternal":false},"hacked ice cream machines"]],["li","What octopus dreams tell us about the ",["a",{"href":"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.wired.com\u002Fstory\u002Fwhat-octopus-dreams-tell-us-about-the-evolution-of-sleep\u002F?itm_campaign=BottomRelatedStories_Sections_1&itm_content=footer-recirc","isExternal":false},"evolution of sleep"]],["li","The lazy gamer’s ",["a",{"href":"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.wired.com\u002Fstory\u002Flazy-gamers-guide-cable-management\u002F?itm_campaign=BottomRelatedStories_Sections_1&itm_content=footer-recirc","isExternal":false},"guide to cable management"]],["li","How to log in to your devices ",["a",{"href":"https:\u002F\u002Fwww.wired.com\u002Fstory\u002Fhow-to-log-in-without-passwords-touchid-face-unlock-windows-hello\u002F?itm_campaign=BottomRelatedStories_Sections_1&itm_content=footer-recirc","isExternal":false},"without passwords"]],["li","Help!
